recordSet.description:
policy-violation-benchmark/VIOLA - 'default' subset

Additional information:
- 3 splits: train, validation, test

name: VIOLA
description:
VIOLA
	


	
		
		Dataset Description
	

VIOLA is a curated benchmark for evaluating whether LLM-based agents comply with
explicit behavioral policies in a multi-agent pipeline. Each example is a single
agent execution trace — a real run of the CUGA multi-agent system on
AppWorld tasks — in which the target
agent's system prompt was modified to induce a specific policy violation using the
contrary instruction injection pattern: rather than removing a… See the full description on the dataset page: https://huggingface.co/datasets/policy-violation-benchmark/VIOLA.
citation: Anonymous. (2026). VIOLA: A Curated Dataset for Evaluating Agent Policy Conformance. NeurIPS 2026 Datasets & Benchmarks Track (under review).
rai:dataCollection: Generated by injecting controlled policy violations into the system prompts of CUGA (Configurable Universal Generic Agent) agents during execution of AppWorld benchmark tasks. Each trace is a complete OpenTelemetry capture of the multi-agent pipeline run. Violations were injected using a 'contrary instruction' pattern: the target policy text is replaced in-place with an actively misdirecting instruction that contradicts it.
rai:dataCollectionType: Synthetic — LLM-generated agent execution traces under controlled prompt manipulation.
rai:dataCollectionRawData: Source tasks: AppWorld benchmark (Stanford NLP). Source agent framework: CUGA multi-agent pipeline (LangGraph-based). Underlying LLM: GPT-4.1 via Azure.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Two-stage LLM-as-judge consensus pipeline using GPT-4.1 at temperature 0. Stage 1 (theoretic violation): verifies the target policy is semantically absent from the modified prompt. Stage 2 (executional violation): verifies the agent response exhibits the violation and assigns hard/soft severity. Each stage runs 3 times per trace; 2/3 agreement required for acceptance. A programmatic pre-check additionally confirms that the contrary instruction was successfully injected.
rai:dataAnnotationPlatform: Programmatic pipeline calling GPT-4.1 via Azure/litellm proxy.
rai:dataAnnotationAnalysis: Stage-1 (theoretic violation) inter-run agreement: 100%. Stage-2 (executional violation) inter-run agreement: 71.6%. Designed-vs-empirical severity reclassification rate: 25.5%. V2 (Prerequisite Bypass) reclassified hard-to-soft in 98% of accepted traces. V6 (Decision Criteria Alteration) reclassified soft-to-hard in 62% of accepted traces. Final acceptance rate: 67.8% (337 violation traces validated of 497 generated). The published dataset includes these 337 violation traces plus 63 clean baseline traces, totaling 400 instances.
rai:dataAnnotatorDemographics: No human annotators in the primary pipeline. All annotation performed by GPT-4.1 (large language model).
rai:dataPreprocessingImputation: Traces failing programmatic pre-check or judge consensus were excluded from the final dataset. 13 (violation × agent) cells were excluded from the compatibility matrix due to systematic incompatibilities, including Pydantic re-serialization issues, baseline outputs that already match the violation, preconditions not met, pre-training override of the removed policy, and policy text appearing in multiple prompt locations. V5 (Output Schema Violation) is fully excluded because it causes parse failures preventing trace extraction.
rai:dataUseCases: Benchmarking policy conformance evaluation methods for LLM agents; training trace classifiers for violation detection; studying agent behavior under adversarial prompt manipulation; evaluating multi-agent monitoring systems against realistic behavioral violations.
rai:dataBiases: (1) Single agent framework: CUGA / LangGraph — violation signatures may not generalize to other multi-agent architectures. (2) Single task suite: AppWorld — domain coverage is narrow. (3) Synthetic injection mechanism: contrary-instruction prompt manipulation may differ from naturally occurring policy violations. (4) Shared-LLM bias: judges (GPT-4.1) and agent (GPT-4.1) share a model family, potentially leading to correlated assessment biases. (5) English-only. (6) Severity skew: the validated dataset is heavily soft-biased (307 soft / 30 hard / 63 clean baseline), reducing statistical power for hard-violation evaluation.
rai:dataLimitations: Small scale: 400 traces across 64 AppWorld tasks; not intended for training large models. Cell sizes uneven (range: 2–47 traces per violation×agent cell). V5 (Output Schema Violation) excluded entirely due to extraction failures. Designed severity differs from empirical severity in 25.5% of cases, indicating that some violations are difficult to predict in advance.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Intended to advance reliable evaluation of LLM-based agents in production deployments by providing a curated benchmark of explicit policy violations with structured labels. Not intended for training agents to commit policy violations. The contrary-instruction injection technique is documented for research transparency and should not be used to attack production systems.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: None. AppWorld tasks operate on synthetic/fictional data (e.g., fictional Spotify accounts, fictional Gmail inboxes, fictional Amazon orders). No real user data, no personally identifiable information.

prov:used.name: CUGA multi-agent pipeline
prov:used.description: Configurable Universal Generic Agent — LangGraph-based multi-agent system.

prov:used.name: AppWorld benchmark
prov:used.description: Stanford NLP benchmark of realistic multi-app tasks.

prov:used.name: GPT-4.1
prov:used.description: OpenAI GPT-4.1 model accessed via Azure as the underlying LLM for both agent execution and judge annotation.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Each trace was generated by injecting a contrary-instruction policy violation into a target agent's system prompt within CUGA, then executing the modified pipeline on an AppWorld task and capturing the full OpenTelemetry trace. Each trace was then validated through a 4-stage pipeline: (1) programmatic pre-check confirming injection, (2) Stage-1 LLM judge (×3) verifying theoretic violation, (3) Stage-2 LLM judge (×3) verifying executional violation and severity, (4) consensus aggregation requiring 2/3 agreement at each stage.
